edges.claim: Syria is explicitly named in European Commission coverage of Gulf-linked humanitarian and development activity, placing Syria within the same Gulf-donor humanitarian landscape in which the UAE is tracked as an active donor; however, no specific UAE aid amounts, programmes or deliveries to Syria are documented in the material.

Syria and the UAE are connected through the EU multilateral humanitarian framework: Syria received the largest allocation (EUR 210 million) of the EU 2026 Middle East humanitarian package, while the UAE maintains a working humanitarian-logistics dialogue with the European Commission (DG ECHO co-hosted Brussels panel, June 2026), making the EU the documented multilateral bridge between the two.

GAP: no evidence in the material documents any UAE-Syria bilateral diplomatic or strategic framework (no embassy activity, ministerial statement, joint communique or agreement between UAE MoFA and Syrian authorities).

GAP: no evidence of any UAE-Syria CEPA or trade relationship; Syria is absent from the documented list of CEPA partners in force (India, Indonesia, Israel, Turkey, Cambodia, Georgia, Costa Rica, Mauritius, Serbia, Jordan) and from those signed but not yet in force.
GAP: no evidence in the material of any ADFD, ADQ or Mubadala sovereign fund deployment, loan, grant facility or project financing into Syria.
GAP: no evidence in the material of any UAE bilateral investment, FDI flow, or commercial investment agreement involving Syria.
GAP: no evidence in the material of any DP World, AD Ports or Masdar energy or infrastructure activity in Syria (no port concession, logistics corridor, or renewable energy project is documented).
GAP: no evidence in the material on a UAE-Syria remittance corridor (no data on Syrian workers in the UAE, remittance volumes, or financial channel arrangements).

The UAE is the largest external donor to Gaza (approximately 50% of all international aid entering Gaza), providing more than $3 billion since 2023 and delivering over 90,000 tons via 8,700 trucks, 80 airdrops and 13 ships under Operations Chivalrous Knight 3, Gallant Knight 3 and Birds of Goodness, with UAE-supported facilities treating 75,000 Gazans.

The UAE and the European Commission maintain a working humanitarian-logistics dialogue: DG ECHO co-hosted a UAE-EU humanitarian supply-chain resilience panel in Brussels on 11 June 2026 attended by more than 80 senior representatives, and both co-signed the Gaza maritime corridor ministerial joint statement.

The UAE and Qatar co-signed the Gaza maritime corridor ministerial joint statement and both co-chaired the 24 September 2025 ministerial meeting coordinating Sudan de-escalation efforts alongside the EU and Kenya.

A 2024 academic study in The International Spectator comparatively analyses Qatar and the UAE as rival Gulf donors in Sudan, indicating competitive dynamics in parallel to their institutional cooperation.

The UAE announced a $30 million Sudan emergency response and pledged $500 million to a Sudan Humanitarian Fund in February 2026, co-chairs the Sudan de-escalation ministerial mechanism, and its MoFA publicly condemned the targeting of a WFP convoy in Sudan while supporting WFP operations there.

The UAE provides direct bilateral food assistance to Kenya, sending 30 tonnes of food aid in February 2026 in response to La Nina-driven drought and food insecurity, while also co-chairing the Sudan de-escalation mechanism with Kenya and holding a signed (not yet in force) CEPA with Kenya.

ADEX, the export-financing arm of the Abu Dhabi Fund for Development, leads the UAE $1 billion AI for Development initiative announced in a G20 context, linking UAE development finance to African digital infrastructure, co-led with the UAE foreign-aid agency.

The UAE contributed $1.5 million to an Asian Development Bank fund promoting agricultural innovation and Asia-Pacific food security in May 2026, channelled via the International Affairs Office at the UAE Presidential Court.

The UAE President issued Federal Decree No. 27 of 2024 establishing the UAE Aid Agency as the federal foreign-aid body, complementing the UAE Aid open-data transparency programme and the Foreign Aid Report 2024, within a foreign-assistance programme running since 1971 with more than $100 billion provided.

The Tony Elumelu Foundation partnered with the UAE Office of Development Affairs and the Khalifa Bin Zayed Al Nahyan Foundation to empower an additional 1,000 young African entrepreneurs; ODA also hosted the International Development Dialogue attended by Theyab bin Mohamed bin Zayed, who chairs the restructured International Humanitarian and Philanthropic Council.
AD Ports Group and Masdar formed a partnership in November 2025 for global offshore wind logistics.

The UAE-India CEPA (signed February 2022) drove bilateral trade to USD 100 billion in FY2024-25 with a USD 200 billion target by 2032, serving as the benchmark template within a CEPA programme that contributed Dh135 billion to the UAE's Dh3 trillion non-oil foreign trade in 2024.
The UAE Ministry of Foreign Affairs condemned the targeting of a WFP humanitarian convoy in Sudan and publicly supports WFP operations there.
The UAE and the EU jointly co-chaired the 24 September 2025 ministerial meeting coordinating Sudan de-escalation efforts, operating a joint co-chair mechanism alongside Qatar and Kenya.
The UAE and Kenya both served as co-chairs in the joint mechanism coordinating international Sudan de-escalation efforts.
The UAE and the United Kingdom co-signed the joint ministerial statement advancing a maritime corridor for humanitarian assistance to Gaza.
The UAE and the United States both joined the multinational ministerial coalition advancing a Gaza humanitarian maritime corridor.
The UAE and Cyprus both joined the joint ministerial statement on advancing the Gaza humanitarian maritime corridor.
Dubai Humanitarian participated in the June 2026 UAE–EU Brussels discussions on global humanitarian supply-chain resilience co-hosted by DG ECHO.

ADEX, the export-financing arm of the Abu Dhabi Fund for Development, leads the UAE's $1 billion AI for Development initiative targeting digital infrastructure across Africa, linking UAE development finance to African digital infrastructure.
The UAE foreign-aid agency, established by Federal Decree No. 27 of 2024, co-leads the $1 billion AI for Development initiative with ADEX, ADFD's export-financing arm.
A 2024 academic study in The International Spectator comparatively analyses Qatar and the UAE as rival Gulf donors in their aid engagement in Sudan.
The Office of Development Affairs at the UAE Presidential Court held an event with the UAE Embassy in Beijing in July 2026.
UN OCHA independently tracks UAE humanitarian flows through its Financial Tracking Service, recording $94,336,382 funded and $133,015,246 paid for 2026, with the UAE tracked as donor #100.
The EU allocated €210 million to Syria within its €458 million 2026 Middle East humanitarian package announced on 16 March 2026, alongside allocations to Palestine, Lebanon, Jordan and Egypt.

Syria is named in European Commission coverage of Gulf-linked humanitarian and development activity, in which the UAE is a named Gulf donor alongside the EU as a major humanitarian donor to the region.
An AllAfrica analysis from May 2026 documents the rise of Emirati aid across Africa.
